skill.name: api-design-principles
skill.description: Principles and checklists for designing and reviewing REST and GraphQL APIs; use when defining or evaluating API contracts (endpoints/schemas), naming, error models, pagination, versioning, and REST vs. GraphQL trade-offs.
skill.summary: Structured principles and checklists for designing and reviewing REST and GraphQL API contracts.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 154 static analysis findings are false positives. This skill is documentation-only markdown files covering REST and GraphQL API design principles. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections match API design documentation content. The 'shell backtick execution' detections match markdown code fences used for example curl commands and GraphQL snippets. No executable code, scripts, or runtime dependencies are present. The skill contains educational API design guidance only, with no security risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm References in Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' in SKILL.md, references/graphql.md, references/rest.md, and references/review-checklist.md. All instances are educational API design documentation content (e.g., Bearer token examples, UUID idempotency keys, base64 cursor examples). No actual cryptographic code exists in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All hits are in markdown documentation files with API design guidance. No cryptographic code exists in this skill. The skill is documentation-only.

low_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution in Markdown
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static analyzer flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in SKILL.md, references/graphql.md, and references/rest.md. These are markdown code fences (triple backticks ` ``` `) used for example curl commands, JSON responses, and GraphQL snippets. No shell execution is possible from markdown documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown code fences are being misidentified as Ruby backtick execution. Files contain no Ruby code or shell scripts.

low_findings.title: C2 Keywords in Example UUID String
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static analyzer flagged C2 keywords at SKILL.md line 101. This line contains an example Idempotency-Key UUID value (2b7b1a2e-7f2b-4c2a-9c2b-0b3b7c9d1a11) in an educational curl example. No command-and-control indicators are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is a UUID example for idempotency key demonstration. UUID hex characters matched a static C2 signature pattern.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance in Curl Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static analyzer flagged 'System reconnaissance' in SKILL.md and references files. These are educational curl command examples showing API authentication patterns (Bearer $TOKEN) and endpoint calls. They are not reconnaissance but instructional documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations are standard API design documentation discussing security, filtering, and naming rules. No actual reconnaissance activity is present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Using Example Domain
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static analyzer flagged hardcoded URLs in SKILL.md. The URLs use api.example.com, which is the IANA-reserved example domain. These are placeholder URLs in educational API design documentation, not actual endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs use example.com domain per IANA RFC 2606 standard for documentation. No actual network requests will be made.

content.user_title: Design Better REST and GraphQL APIs
content.value_statement: Designing consistent, maintainable APIs is difficult without clear principles and review processes. This skill provides structured guidelines, design patterns, and review checklists for REST and GraphQL API contracts, helping teams produce reliable and evolvable APIs.
actual_capabilities: Provides structured REST API design principles including resource modeling, HTTP verb semantics, and status codes
Provides structured GraphQL schema design guidance including type modeling, queries, mutations, and pagination
Includes a review checklist for validating API completeness and identifying gaps and risks
Covers cross-cutting concerns: error handling, pagination, versioning, deprecation, and security
Offers guidance on choosing between REST and GraphQL for different use cases and team needs
Documents idempotency, caching, long-running operations, and concurrency control patterns
limitations: Does not generate working API code or server implementations
Does not automatically enforce or validate API designs against rules
Does not cover gRPC, WebSocket, or event-driven API patterns
Does not provide language-specific client SDK generation or OpenAPI spec generation

use_cases.title: Design a New REST API
use_cases.description: A backend engineer creating a RESTful API for a project management application uses the principles to define resources, endpoints, error models, and pagination strategy.

use_cases.title: Review an Existing GraphQL Schema
use_cases.description: A platform team lead reviews an evolving GraphQL schema for naming consistency, field ownership, nullability rules, and deprecation practices.

use_cases.title: Standardize API Practices Across Teams
use_cases.description: An API platform team adopts the review checklist as part of their API design review process to ensure consistent error handling, versioning, and security across services.

prompt_templates.title: Design REST Endpoints
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to design REST API endpoints for [describe domain]. Help me model the resources, choose HTTP verbs, define URL patterns, and design consistent error responses.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a new REST API from scratch

prompt_templates.title: Review API Contract for Consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this API contract for [describe API]. Check for naming consistency, HTTP semantics, error model completeness, pagination, versioning strategy, and security considerations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluating an existing API design before implementation

prompt_templates.title: Choose Between REST and GraphQL
prompt_templates.prompt: I am deciding between REST and GraphQL for [describe project]. Help me evaluate trade-offs based on my requirements: [list requirements and constraints].
prompt_templates.scenario: Architecture decision for a new service

prompt_templates.title: Design GraphQL Schema with Pagination
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GraphQL schema for [describe domain]. Include types, queries, mutations, input types, pagination using the Connection pattern, and error handling.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a new GraphQL API for multiple client applications

output_examples.input: Design a REST API for a task management system
output_examples.output: Provides a structured contract outline including base URL, resource definitions, endpoint list with HTTP methods, request and response examples with error models, and a pagination strategy using cursor-based pagination.

output_examples.input: Review my GraphQL schema for naming consistency
output_examples.output: Checks naming conventions, field ownership, separation of input and output types, nullability rules, Query versus Mutation boundaries, pagination pattern usage, and deprecation annotations.

output_examples.input: What versioning strategy should I use for my API?
output_examples.output: Outlines options including URL prefix versioning, header-based versioning, and media type negotiation. Provides trade-offs for each approach and recommendations based on API maturity and client requirements.

best_practices: Use plural nouns for REST resource collections and maintain consistent naming conventions throughout the API
Separate GraphQL input types from output types to keep the schema explicit and prevent accidental field exposure
Document idempotency behavior for all mutating operations, especially for payment and order workflows
anti_patterns: Embedding action verbs in REST URLs instead of using proper HTTP verbs and sub-resources for actions
Returning partial GraphQL query results without explaining which fields failed and why they failed
Skipping pagination on endpoints that return lists, which leads to unbounded response sizes and performance issues

faq.question: Does this skill generate code for my API?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides design guidance, patterns, and review checklists. It does not generate server code, SDKs, or configuration files.

faq.question: Can I use this for gRPC or WebSocket APIs?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on REST and GraphQL. It does not cover gRPC, WebSocket, or event-driven API design patterns.

faq.question: How do I use the review checklist?
faq.answer: Reference the review-checklist.md document after designing your API. Check each item against your design and document any gaps or risks you find.

faq.question: Does this skill support OpenAPI or GraphQL schema formats?
faq.answer: This skill provides principles for manual use. It does not generate or validate OpenAPI specs or GraphQL SDL files automatically.

faq.question: Should I use REST or GraphQL for my new project?
faq.answer: The skill helps you evaluate trade-offs. REST works well for resource-oriented CRUD operations. GraphQL is better for multiple clients with varied data requirements.

faq.question: How do I handle API versioning with this skill?
faq.answer: The skill documents URL prefix versioning, header-based versioning, and media type negotiation approaches. You choose based on your deployment and client upgrade needs.
